part_title: Section Three: Monitoring, Early Detection, and Warning

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

Regular monitoring is the foundation linking knowledge of the pest to control decisions. Genuine integrated pest management cannot be implemented on the basis of guesswork, observation of damage only after it has spread, or a fixed spray program applied every few days regardless of field conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Regular monitoring is the foundation linking knowledge of the pest to control decisions. Genuine [[term:ipm]]integrated pest management[[/term]] cannot be implemented on the basis of guesswork, observation of damage only after it has spread, or a fixed spray program applied every few days regardless of field conditions.

items.text: Monitoring aims to detect Tuta absoluta before larvae reach growing points, stems, and fruits; determine population trends; identify the stages present in the field; and evaluate whether preventive, biological, and chemical measures are producing the required results.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring aims to detect Tuta absoluta before larvae reach growing points, stems, and fruits; determine population trends; identify the stages present in the field; and evaluate whether preventive, biological, and chemical measures are producing the required results.

items.text: Early detection is particularly important because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Early detection is particularly important because:

items: Eggs are small and difficult to detect without systematic inspection.
Newly hatched larvae enter plant tissues shortly after hatching.
Larvae inside mines are less exposed to control measures.
Fruit infestation cannot be reversed after it occurs.
Generations overlap rapidly under favorable conditions.
Increased moth catches may precede visible damage.
Neighboring old fields may cause sudden immigration waves.
Infestation may begin with transplants or along one field edge.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Eggs are small and difficult to detect without systematic inspection.
[[prepared]]Newly hatched larvae enter plant tissues shortly after hatching.
[[prepared]]Larvae inside [[term:mines]]mines[[/term]] are less exposed to control measures.
[[prepared]]Fruit infestation cannot be reversed after it occurs.
[[prepared]]Increased moth catches may precede visible damage.
[[prepared]]Neighboring old fields may cause sudden immigration waves.
[[prepared]]Infestation may begin with transplants or along one field edge.

items.text: Monitoring is not limited to pheromone traps. It depends on integrating several sources of information, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring is not limited to [[term:pheromone-trap]]pheromone traps[[/term]]. It depends on integrating several sources of information, including:

items: 6. Inspection of growing points and flowers.
10. Monitoring neighboring fields and immigration sources.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]6. Inspection of growing points and flowers.
[[prepared]]10. Monitoring neighboring fields and immigration sources.

items.text: Objectives of Monitoring
A monitoring program aims to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A monitoring program aims to:

items: Determine the date of first detection.
Detect the beginning of moth flight.
Identify the most exposed field edges and sectors.
Determine whether infestation was introduced with transplants or came from an external source.
Distinguish old mines from recent mines.
Identify the beginning of growing-point or fruit infestation.
Assess the role of natural enemies.
Detect reduced insecticide efficacy or possible resistance.
Predict appearance of the next generation.
Document infestation history throughout the season.
Provide early warning to neighboring fields.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Identify the most exposed field edges and sectors.
[[prepared]]Determine whether infestation was introduced with transplants or came from an external source.
[[prepared]]Identify the beginning of growing-point or fruit infestation.
[[prepared]]Detect reduced insecticide efficacy or possible resistance.

items.text: Difference Between Detection, Monitoring, Assessment, and Warning
Detection
Detection
Detection aims to determine whether the pest is present or absent.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Detection aims to determine whether the pest is present or absent.

items.text: It is used particularly:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is used particularly:

items: At the beginning of the season.

items.text: Initial detection may be confirmed by finding:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Initial detection may be confirmed by finding:

items.text: Monitoring
Monitoring
Monitoring means collecting data periodically to determine pest-population trends.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring means collecting data periodically to determine pest-population trends.

items.text: It answers questions such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It answers questions such as:

items.text: Infestation Assessment
Infestation Assessment
This means measuring infestation on plants through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This means measuring infestation on plants through:

items.text: Early Warning
Early Warning
Early warning is the use of trap results, plant inspection, temperature, and surrounding information to predict increasing risk before major economic loss occurs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Early warning is the use of trap results, plant inspection, temperature, and surrounding information to predict increasing risk before major economic loss occurs.

items.text: Monitoring Is Not Direct Control
Pheromone traps are used at low numerical density for monitoring, whereas they are used at high density for mass trapping.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromone traps are used at low numerical density for monitoring, whereas they are used at high density for mass trapping.

items.text: The two systems must be distinguished.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The two systems must be distinguished.

items.text: Monitoring Traps
Their purpose is to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their purpose is to:

items: Identify the timing of inspection and intervention.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Identify the timing of inspection and intervention.

items.text: They are not expected to reduce the pest population substantially when used alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They are not expected to reduce the pest population substantially when used alone.

items.text: Mass-Trapping Traps
A large number of traps is used to capture a substantial proportion of males and reduce mating opportunities.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A large number of traps is used to capture a substantial proportion of males and reduce mating opportunities.

items.text: Published mass-trapping densities may range between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Published mass-trapping densities may range between:

items.text: 20 and 50 traps per hectare or more
Depending on trap type, production system, and level of enclosure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Depending on trap type, production system, and level of enclosure.

items.text: These densities should not be used as monitoring densities.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These densities should not be used as monitoring densities.

items.text: Pheromone Traps
Pheromone traps use a synthetic sex pheromone that mimics the odor released by females to attract males.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromone traps use a synthetic sex pheromone that mimics the odor released by females to attract males.

items.text: Pheromone traps primarily capture male Tuta absoluta moths.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromone traps primarily capture male Tuta absoluta moths.

items.text: Their advantages include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their advantages include:

items: Ability to detect the pest at low population densities.
Continuous operation day and night without labor.
Allowing comparison of activity among weeks.
Detecting the beginning of flight before severe damage appears.
Leaving no residues on the crop.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability to detect the pest at low population densities.
[[prepared]]Continuous operation day and night without labor.
[[prepared]]Detecting the beginning of flight before severe damage appears.

items.text: However, they do not directly measure:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, they do not directly measure:

items.text: Types of Traps Used for Monitoring
Delta Sticky Trap
Delta Sticky Trap
It consists of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It consists of:

items.text: Its advantages include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its advantages include:

items: Possibility of retaining moths for examination.

items.text: Its disadvantages include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its disadvantages include:

items: Saturation of the sticky card with insects.
Reduced adhesive performance under heat and dust.
Difficult counting at high pest densities.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Saturation of the sticky card with insects.
[[prepared]]Reduced adhesive performance under heat and dust.

items.text: Water Trap
It consists of a container holding:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It consists of a container holding:

items: A small quantity of a surface-tension-reducing material, such as unscented soap.
A support holding the pheromone lure above the water.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A small quantity of a surface-tension-reducing material, such as unscented soap.
[[prepared]]A support holding the pheromone lure above the water.

items.text: Its advantages include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its advantages include:

items.text: It requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It requires:

items: Prevention of filling with dust and leaves.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevention of filling with dust and leaves.

items.text: Funnel or Bucket Traps
Moths enter through an opening into a container from which escape is difficult.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moths enter through an opening into a container from which escape is difficult.

items.text: Their efficacy varies according to design, pheromone, and installation site.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their efficacy varies according to design, pheromone, and installation site.

items.text: Light Traps
Light attracts males, females, and other insects.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Light attracts males, females, and other insects.

items.text: Light traps may be used in research or specialized systems, but they:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Light traps may be used in research or specialized systems, but they:

items: May capture natural enemies and beneficial insects.
Are not a standard substitute for pheromone traps in routine field monitoring.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]May capture natural enemies and beneficial insects.
[[prepared]]Are not a standard substitute for pheromone traps in routine field monitoring.

items.text: Timing of Trap Installation
Monitoring traps should be installed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring traps should be installed:

items: Approximately one to two weeks before transplanting when possible.
They may remain for a period after harvest when immigration sources are being monitored.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Approximately one to two weeks before transplanting when possible.
[[prepared]]They may remain for a period after harvest when immigration sources are being monitored.

items.text: Early installation provides benefits including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Early installation provides benefits including:

items: Detecting moth presence before transplants enter the field.
Comparing pressure before and after planting.
Preventing delayed initiation of plant inspection.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Detecting moth presence before transplants enter the field.

items.text: Trap installation should not be delayed until mines appear.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap installation should not be delayed until mines appear.

items.text: Monitoring-Trap Density
Recommendations vary according to field area, shape, and surrounding conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Recommendations vary according to field area, shape, and surrounding conditions.

items.text: References for open fields indicate approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]References for open fields indicate approximately:

items.text: 2–3 monitoring traps per hectare.
Because one feddan is approximately 0.42 hectare, using:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Because one feddan is approximately 0.42 hectare, using:

items.text: One trap per feddan
Is equivalent to approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Is equivalent to approximately:

items.text: 2.38 traps per hectare
And represents a practical monitoring rate within the commonly used range for open fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And represents a practical monitoring rate within the commonly used range for open fields.

items.text: This number is not rigid. Additional traps may be needed when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This number is not rigid. Additional traps may be needed when:

items: More than one direction presents risk.
Edges are adjacent to old fields.
Planting dates differ markedly within the site.
Several cultivars or separate sectors are present.
One trap cannot adequately represent the field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Planting dates differ markedly within the site.
[[prepared]]Several cultivars or separate sectors are present.
[[prepared]]One trap cannot adequately represent the field.

items.text: One trap per feddan for monitoring must not be confused with the high densities used for mass trapping.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One trap per feddan for monitoring must not be confused with the high densities used for mass trapping.

items.text: Distribution of Traps Within the Field
Traps should be distributed to represent both the field and risk sources.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps should be distributed to represent both the field and risk sources.

items.text: Distribution includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Distribution includes:

items: One trap inside the field away from edges.
One or more traps along high-risk edges.
A trap near the transplant-collection area during planting.
Traps in different sectors when clear variation exists.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]One trap inside the field away from edges.
[[prepared]]One or more traps along high-risk edges.
[[prepared]]A trap near the transplant-collection area during planting.
[[prepared]]Traps in different sectors when clear variation exists.

items.text: All traps should not be placed on one edge because this does not represent the field interior.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]All traps should not be placed on one edge because this does not represent the field interior.

items.text: Traps should also not be placed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps should also not be placed:

items: Directly beside a heavily dusted road.
Beneath a sprinkler or water source.
Inside a closed canopy that blocks air movement.
Near another pheromone source in a way that causes interference.
In a location inaccessible for counting and maintenance.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Inside a closed canopy that blocks air movement.
[[prepared]]Near another pheromone source in a way that causes interference.
[[prepared]]In a location inaccessible for counting and maintenance.

items.text: Trap Height
Trap height changes as plants grow.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap height changes as plants grow.

items.text: In practice:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In practice:

items: Before planting: approximately 20 cm above the soil surface.
After transplanting: near the top of the canopy.
As plants grow: traps are raised gradually.
In many cases: height does not exceed approximately 50–60 cm in determinate field tomatoes.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Before planting: approximately 20 cm above the soil surface.
[[prepared]]After transplanting: near the top of the canopy.
[[prepared]]As plants grow: traps are raised gradually.
[[prepared]]In many cases: height does not exceed approximately 50–60 cm in determinate field tomatoes.

items.text: Some experiments have shown higher male catches when traps were placed at heights not exceeding 60 cm compared with higher positions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some experiments have shown higher male catches when traps were placed at heights not exceeding 60 cm compared with higher positions.

items.text: The lure and trap should remain within the airflow zone without being buried inside foliage or raised far above the crop.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The lure and trap should remain within the airflow zone without being buried inside foliage or raised far above the crop.

items.text: Trap Numbering and Fixed Locations
Each trap should receive a permanent identifier, such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Each trap should receive a permanent identifier, such as:

items.text: The following should be recorded for each trap:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded for each trap:

items: Number of moths at each reading.

items.text: Traps should not be moved repeatedly because this disrupts temporal comparisons.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps should not be moved repeatedly because this disrupts temporal comparisons.

items.text: When relocation is necessary, record:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When relocation is necessary, record:

items.text: Handling Pheromone Lures
The following should be observed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be observed:

items: Store lures according to manufacturer instructions.
Avoid exposure to high temperatures before use.
Do not open the package before installation.
Use gloves or clean forceps when possible.
Do not handle different pheromone types with the same tool.
Do not place the lure directly on an adhesive surface that covers it.
Replace the lure according to its specified service life.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Avoid exposure to high temperatures before use.
[[prepared]]Do not open the package before installation.
[[prepared]]Use gloves or clean forceps when possible.
[[prepared]]Do not handle different pheromone types with the same tool.
[[prepared]]Do not place the lure directly on an adhesive surface that covers it.
[[prepared]]Replace the lure according to its specified service life.

items.text: Many sources recommend replacing monitoring lures after approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Many sources recommend replacing monitoring lures after approximately:

items.text: 4–6 weeks
However, actual longevity varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, actual longevity varies according to:

items.text: High temperatures increase pheromone-release rate and may shorten the effective life of some lures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High temperatures increase pheromone-release rate and may shorten the effective life of some lures.

items.text: Replacement of Sticky Cards and Water
Sticky Cards
They should be replaced when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They should be replaced when:

items.text: Weekly replacement may be required in very dusty areas or under high moth density.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weekly replacement may be required in very dusty areas or under high moth density.

items.text: Water Traps
Water should be checked regularly and replenished when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Water should be checked regularly and replenished when:

items: The trap becomes filled with soil or insects.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The trap becomes filled with soil or insects.

items.text: A limited amount of unscented soap or a suitable oil may be added to reduce surface tension and prevent moth escape.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A limited amount of unscented soap or a suitable oil may be added to reduce surface tension and prevent moth escape.

items.text: Frequency of Trap Reading
Traps are generally read:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps are generally read:

items.text: At least once weekly.
Reading frequency should increase to twice weekly when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reading frequency should increase to twice weekly when:

items: The crop enters flowering and fruiting.
A rapid response to an immigration wave is being evaluated.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A rapid response to an immigration wave is being evaluated.

items.text: Readings should preferably be taken on the same day each week so that time intervals remain comparable.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Readings should preferably be taken on the same day each week so that time intervals remain comparable.

items.text: Recording Trap Catches
The actual number in each individual trap should be recorded.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The actual number in each individual trap should be recorded.

items.text: Terms such as the following are insufficient:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Terms such as the following are insufficient:

items.text: When reading intervals differ, calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When reading intervals differ, calculate:

items.text: Mean daily catch = Number of moths captured ÷ Number of days since the previous reading
Example:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Example:

items.text: If a trap captures 35 moths in seven days:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If a trap captures 35 moths in seven days:

items.text: 35 ÷ 7 = 5 moths per trap per day.
If the interval is five days, the result should not be compared directly with a seven-day reading until standardized on a daily basis.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If the interval is five days, the result should not be compared directly with a seven-day reading until standardized on a daily basis.

items.text: Interpretation of Trap Counts
Moth capture indicates active males in the area, but by itself does not prove economic loss.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Moth capture indicates active males in the area, but by itself does not prove economic loss.

items.text: Historical advisory levels have included:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Historical advisory levels have included:

headers: Number of Males per Trap per Week
prepared_headers: [[prepared]]Number of Males per Trap per Week

items.text: However, these values:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, these values:

items: Should not be used alone to trigger spraying.
Are affected by trap and pheromone type.
Are affected by trap location and height.
Are affected by immigration and wind.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Should not be used alone to trigger spraying.
[[prepared]]Are affected by trap and pheromone type.
[[prepared]]Are affected by trap location and height.

items.text: These levels should be used as advisory signals for intensifying inspection, not as fixed commands for chemical intervention.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These levels should be used as advisory signals for intensifying inspection, not as fixed commands for chemical intervention.

items.text: Why Trap Catches Do Not Always Correspond to Damage
High trap catches may occur without high plant infestation because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High trap catches may occur without high plant infestation because of:

items: Immigration of males from a neighboring field.
Failure of moths to establish within the field.
Presence of a highly attractive trap.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Immigration of males from a neighboring field.
[[prepared]]Failure of moths to establish within the field.

items.text: High damage may occur with low trap catches because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High damage may occur with low trap catches because of:

items: Larvae resulting from an earlier infestation.
A hatching wave occurring after male flight declines.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A hatching wave occurring after male flight declines.

items.text: Trap data must therefore always be linked with plant inspection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Trap data must therefore always be linked with plant inspection.

items.text: Trend Is More Important Than a Single Reading
A sequence of readings is more valuable than a number from one week.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A sequence of readings is more valuable than a number from one week.

items.text: Important trends include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Important trends include:

items.text: Gradual Increase
This may indicate establishment of a population within the area.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may indicate establishment of a population within the area.

items.text: Sudden Surge
This may indicate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may indicate:

items: Emergence of a generation from pupae.
Cessation of spraying in an old field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Cessation of spraying in an old field.

items.text: Decline
This may result from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may result from:

items.text: Every decline should not be assumed to represent success without reviewing trap condition and plant infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Every decline should not be assumed to represent success without reviewing trap condition and plant infestation.

items.text: Plant Inspection
Direct plant inspection is the method that confirms whether captured moths have resulted in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Direct plant inspection is the method that confirms whether captured moths have resulted in:

items.text: Inspection should be:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Inspection should be:

items: Conducted separately for field edges and the interior.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Conducted separately for field edges and the interior.

items.text: Division of the Field into Sectors
The field should be divided into homogeneous sectors according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The field should be divided into homogeneous sectors according to:

items.text: A large heterogeneous field should not be treated as a single unit.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A large heterogeneous field should not be treated as a single unit.

items.text: In large areas, the site may be divided into units of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In large areas, the site may be divided into units of approximately:

items.text: 2–5 feddans per sector
Depending on the degree of uniformity.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Depending on the degree of uniformity.

items.text: Each sector should have:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Each sector should have:

items.text: Operational Sampling Model
The following model may be used in small and medium-sized open fields:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following model may be used in small and medium-sized open fields:

items: Select 5–10 points distributed within the sector.
Examine no fewer than approximately 50 plants per homogeneous sector.
Add 10–20 plants along the highest-risk edge.
Change the individual plants examined each week while maintaining the same overall distribution route.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Examine no fewer than approximately 50 plants per homogeneous sector.
[[prepared]]Change the individual plants examined each week while maintaining the same overall distribution route.

items.text: This model is not a fixed international threshold, but an operational protocol whose sample size should increase when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This model is not a fixed international threshold, but an operational protocol whose sample size should increase when:

items: More than one immigration source exists.

items.text: Inspection Route
The inspection route should cover:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The inspection route should cover:

items.text: Possible routes include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Possible routes include:

items: A fixed grid of sampling points.
Separate routes for field edges and the interior.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Separate routes for field edges and the interior.

items.text: Inspection should not be limited to a single row, and apparently healthy plants should not be selected exclusively.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Inspection should not be limited to a single row, and apparently healthy plants should not be selected exclusively.

items.text: Plant Parts Examined
The following should be examined on each plant:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be examined on each plant:

items.text: Young Growth

items: The last three fully expanded leaves.

items.text: Young leaves are important sites for egg detection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Young leaves are important sites for egg detection.

items.text: Middle Section
Lower Section
Fruits
Beginning at fruit set, inspect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Beginning at fruit set, inspect:

items.text: Egg Monitoring
The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items: Presence of blackened or parasitized eggs.

items.text: Priority should be given to examining:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Priority should be given to examining:

items: The last three fully expanded leaves.

items.text: Egg counting requires:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Egg counting requires:

items.text: Viable eggs should not be combined with parasitized eggs or empty shells.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Viable eggs should not be combined with parasitized eggs or empty shells.

items.text: Mine Monitoring
The following should be recorded for each mine:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded for each mine:

items: Does it contain a live larva?
What is the approximate larval size?
Was the damage caused by Tuta or another insect?
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Was the damage caused by Tuta or another insect?

items.text: The combined total of old and recent mines should not be used as the sole indicator.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The combined total of old and recent mines should not be used as the sole indicator.

items.text: It is preferable to record:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is preferable to record:

items: Percentage of plants bearing active mines.

items.text: Distinguishing Active and Old Mines
An empty old mine does not require direct targeting, but should be recorded to assess previous damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An empty old mine does not require direct targeting, but should be recorded to assess previous damage.

items.text: Monitoring Larval Instars
Estimating larval instar helps in selecting the intervention.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Estimating larval instar helps in selecting the intervention.

items.text: Early Instars
They are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They are:

items: More exposed to biological and selective insecticides.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]More exposed to biological and selective insecticides.

items.text: Third and Fourth Instars
They are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They are:

items: More likely to enter growing points, stems, and fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]More likely to enter growing points, stems, and fruits.

items.text: Simultaneous presence of multiple instars indicates:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Simultaneous presence of multiple instars indicates:

items: The inadequacy of relying on a single treatment.
The need to monitor new hatching.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The inadequacy of relying on a single treatment.

items.text: Monitoring Growing Points and Buds
The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items: Percentage of plants with infested growing points.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Percentage of plants with infested growing points.

items.text: This indicator becomes particularly important:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This indicator becomes particularly important:

items: When advanced larval instars are present.

items.text: Fruit Monitoring
Fruit inspection should begin at fruit set and should not be delayed until coloration begins.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fruit inspection should begin at fruit set and should not be delayed until coloration begins.

items.text: The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items: Presence of infestation beneath the calyx.

items.text: Calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calculate:

items.text: Infested fruits (%) = Number of infested fruits ÷ Total number of fruits examined × 100
For processing tomatoes, also calculate:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]For processing tomatoes, also calculate:

items.text: Weight loss (%) = Weight of infested and rejected fruits ÷ Total sample weight × 100
Inspection of Field Edges and Hotspots
Field edges should be inspected independently because the field average may conceal a severe focus.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field edges should be inspected independently because the field average may conceal a severe focus.

items.text: Hotspots include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Hotspots include:

items: The edge facing an old field.
Sectors where infestation occurred during the previous week.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Sectors where infestation occurred during the previous week.

items.text: The overall field average should not be used alone to make decisions about a focus that may later spread.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The overall field average should not be used alone to make decisions about a focus that may later spread.

items.text: Monitoring Natural Enemies
Natural enemies should be recorded alongside the pest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Natural enemies should be recorded alongside the pest.

items.text: Monitoring includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring includes:

items: Larvae killed by fungi or bacteria.

items.text: This information affects decision-making because strong biological activity may limit pest population growth, whereas a broad-spectrum insecticide may remove this natural regulation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This information affects decision-making because strong biological activity may limit pest population growth, whereas a broad-spectrum insecticide may remove this natural regulation.

items.text: Early-Warning Indicators
Early-warning indicators include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Early-warning indicators include:

items: 1. First moth captured in a trap.
2. Increased trap catches for two consecutive weeks.
3. Increased catches along a specific field edge.
4. Presence of an old field where control has stopped.
5. Sudden removal of a neighboring infested crop.
8. Appearance of the first active mine.
9. Detection of the first larva in a growing point.
10. Beginning of fruit set while advanced larvae are present.
11. Increase in temperatures favorable for development.
12. Decline of natural enemies after insecticide use.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. First moth captured in a trap.
[[prepared]]2. Increased trap catches for two consecutive weeks.
[[prepared]]3. Increased catches along a specific field edge.
[[prepared]]4. Presence of an old field where control has stopped.
[[prepared]]5. Sudden removal of a neighboring infested crop.
[[prepared]]8. Appearance of the first active mine.
[[prepared]]9. Detection of the first larva in a growing point.
[[prepared]]10. Beginning of fruit set while advanced larvae are present.
[[prepared]]11. Increase in temperatures favorable for development.
[[prepared]]12. Decline of natural enemies after insecticide use.

items.text: Risk increases as more indicators occur simultaneously.

items.text: Use of Degree-Days in Warning Systems
Temperature data can be used to predict the rate of generation development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Temperature data can be used to predict the rate of generation development.

Degree-days are then accumulated.

items.text: This model should not be used alone to determine spray timing, but to assist in:

Intensifying inspection at the appropriate time.

items.text: Thermal models vary among studies and regions and should therefore be calibrated using local field data.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Thermal models vary among studies and regions and should therefore be calibrated using local field data.

items.text: Basic Monitoring Equations
Percentage of Infested Plants
Number of plants with active infestation ÷ Total number of plants examined × 100
Percentage of Infested Leaves
Number of leaves with active mines ÷ Total number of leaves examined × 100
Mean Number of Larvae per Plant
Total number of live larvae ÷ Number of plants examined
Percentage of Infested Growing Points
Number of plants with infested growing points ÷ Total number of plants examined × 100
Percentage of Infested Fruits
Number of infested fruits ÷ Total number of fruits examined × 100
Trap-Catch Rate of Change
Current-week catch ÷ Previous-week catch
If the result is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If the result is:

items.text: This rate must be interpreted together with plant inspection and lure condition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This rate must be interpreted together with plant inspection and lure condition.

From Transplanting to Three Weeks

items: Search for infestation introduced with transplants.

items.text: Vegetative Growth
Flowering and Fruit Set

items: Increase inspection of growing points and clusters.
Prevent larvae from moving into fruits.

items.text: Fruit Filling

items: Focus on the area beneath the calyx.
Increase the number of fruits examined.

items.text: Before and During Harvest

items: Evaluate whether further intervention remains necessary.

items.text: After Harvest

items: Continue traps for an appropriate period.

items.text: Evaluation of Intervention Results
A treatment should not be judged immediately on the following day.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A treatment should not be judged immediately on the following day.

items.text: Evaluation should be conducted according to the speed of action of the method used and should include:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be distinguished:

items.text: Success should not be measured by disappearance of old mines because mines remain visible on leaves after larvae die.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Success should not be measured by disappearance of old mines because mines remain visible on leaves after larvae die.

items.text: Interfield Warning System
A simple data-sharing system can be established among growers or between a company and contracted fields.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A simple data-sharing system can be established among growers or between a company and contracted fields.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Priority warnings should be issued when:

items: Spraying stops in a neighboring source field.
Efficacy of an insecticide group declines.
An infestation wave appears in several neighboring fields.

items.text: Plant Data
Biological Status
Risk Level
Action
Common Monitoring Errors
Error One: Depending on One Trap in an Unrepresentative Location
It may fail to detect trends along other edges or sectors.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may fail to detect trends along other edges or sectors.

items.text: Error Two: Moving a Trap Without Recording the Change
This invalidates comparison among readings.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This invalidates comparison among readings.

items.text: Error Three: Comparing Readings Taken Over Different Time Intervals
Results should be converted to moths per day or reading duration should be standardized.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Results should be converted to moths per day or reading duration should be standardized.

items.text: Error Four: Failing to Replace the Lure
The population may appear low while the pheromone is weak.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The population may appear low while the pheromone is weak.

items.text: Error Five: Treating Trap Counts as an Independent Spray Threshold
Traps measure males and do not directly measure damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps measure males and do not directly measure damage.

items.text: Error Six: Counting Old Mines as Active Infestation
This may lead to unnecessary spraying.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may lead to unnecessary spraying.

items.text: Error Seven: Inspecting Only the Field Interior
Infestation may begin along the edges.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Infestation may begin along the edges.

items.text: Error Eight: Inspecting Only Field Edges
Infestation introduced with transplants may appear internally.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Infestation introduced with transplants may appear internally.

items.text: Error Nine: Failing to Open Mines
Tuta infestation may be confused with leaf-miner flies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta infestation may be confused with leaf-miner flies.

items.text: Error Ten: Ignoring Growing Points and Fruits
Infestation there may be more serious than visible leaf damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Infestation there may be more serious than visible leaf damage.

items.text: Error Eleven: Ignoring Natural Enemies
This may result in unnecessary or harmful chemical decisions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may result in unnecessary or harmful chemical decisions.

items.text: Error Twelve: Judging Treatment Success from Old Mines
Old mines remain visible even after successful control.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Old mines remain visible even after successful control.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Combining Trap Counts Without Separating Locations
A strong immigration source may disappear within the general average.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A strong immigration source may disappear within the general average.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Failing to Monitor Neighboring Fields
A new crop may be surprised by a severe immigration wave.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A new crop may be surprised by a severe immigration wave.

items.text: Indicators of a Successful Monitoring Program
A monitoring program is successful when it achieves:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A monitoring program is successful when it achieves:

items: Detection of eggs before mines increase.
Prevention or reduction of fruit infestation.
Detection of foci before they spread.
Provision of warnings to neighboring fields.

items.text: Summary of Section Three
Monitoring and early detection form the core information system of integrated management of Tuta absoluta.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Monitoring and early detection form the core information system of integrated management of Tuta absoluta.

items.text: Pheromone traps are used to detect male moths and monitor flight activity, but by themselves do not measure the level of economic damage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pheromone traps are used to detect male moths and monitor flight activity, but by themselves do not measure the level of economic damage.

items.text: One monitoring trap per feddan may be adopted in open-field tomatoes as a practical rate equivalent to approximately 2.38 traps per hectare, with additional traps placed along edges or near risk sources when necessary.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One monitoring trap per feddan may be adopted in open-field tomatoes as a practical rate equivalent to approximately 2.38 traps per hectare, with additional traps placed along edges or near risk sources when necessary.

items.text: Traps should be read at least weekly, with more frequent readings when risk increases, and each trap should be recorded separately.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Traps should be read at least weekly, with more frequent readings when risk increases, and each trap should be recorded separately.

items.text: True assessment depends on plant inspection to determine:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]True assessment depends on plant inspection to determine:

items.text: Weekly catches of 1–3, 4–30, or more than 30 moths do not represent fixed economic thresholds. They are historical advisory levels that must be interpreted together with plant condition.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Weekly catches of 1–3, 4–30, or more than 30 moths do not represent fixed economic thresholds. They are historical advisory levels that must be interpreted together with plant condition.

items.text: The direction of change between weeks is more useful than a single reading, and old infestation must be distinguished from active infestation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The direction of change between weeks is more useful than a single reading, and [[term:infestation-status]]old infestation[[/term]] must be distinguished from active infestation.

items.text: Integrating traps, plant inspection, temperature, and information from neighboring fields enables early warning and intervention before larvae reach fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Integrating traps, plant inspection, temperature, and information from neighboring fields enables early warning and intervention before larvae reach fruits.
